khan_exercises.title: Identifying subjects and predicates
khan_exercises.description: Identify subject and predicate as parts of the sentences.

items.question_content:
**Choose whether the bolded part of the sentence is the subject or the predicate.** 

> **The cowboys’ singing** echoed through the canyon.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A clause has two main parts:

1. The **subject** is the main person or thing in the clause performing an action.
2. The **predicate** contains the verb and describes the action that the subject is performing. It's the *rest* of the sentence that's *not* the subject.

Typically, clauses start with the subject and finish with the predicate.
Since the bolded part of the sentence tells us *who* is performing the action, it's the **subject**.

items.question_content:
**Choose whether the bolded part of the sentence is the subject or the predicate.** 

> The noble horse **washed its mane in the waterfall.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A clause has two main parts:

1. The **subject** is the main person or thing in the clause performing an action.
2. The **predicate** contains the verb and describes the action that the subject is performing. It's the *rest* of the sentence that's *not* the subject.

Typically, clauses start with the subject and finish with the predicate.
Since the bolded part of the sentence tells us *what the action is*, it's the **predicate**.

items.question_content:

Typically, clauses start with the subject and finish with the predicate.
The subject of the sentence (the person doing the action) is **"Simone"**.

khan_exercises.title: Identifying subjects, direct objects, and indirect objects
khan_exercises.description: Learn to identify the subject and direct/indirect objects of sentences.
topic_path: Identifying subjects, direct objects, and indirect objects

items.question_content:
**Choose whether the bolded part of the sentence is the subject, direct object, or indirect object.**

> **We** have to pick up strawberries on the way to the picnic.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Nouns can relate to verbs in three main ways:

* A **subject** is the noun that *performs* the verb; it's the *who*.

 *Adam baked.*

 **Adam** is the **subject**.

* A **direct object** is the noun being *acted* upon; it's the *what*

 *Adam baked a cake.*

 **Cake** is the **direct object**.

* An **indirect object** is the noun *receiving* the action; it's the *for whom*.

 *Adam baked Martha a cake.*

 **Martha** is the **indirect object**.
The bolded noun is *doing* the action, so it's the **subject**.

items.question_content:
**Choose whether the bolded part of the sentence is the subject, direct object, or indirect object.**

> I threw the **ball** to Yvonne.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Nouns can relate to verbs in three main ways:

* A **subject** is the noun that *performs* the verb; it's the *who*.

 *Adam baked.*

 **Adam** is the **subject**.

* A **direct object** is the noun being *acted* upon; it's the *what*

 *Adam baked a cake.*

 **Cake** is the **direct object**.

* An **indirect object** is the noun *receiving* the action; it's the *for whom*.

 *Adam baked Martha a cake.*

 **Martha** is the **indirect object**.
The bolded noun is being *acted upon* (it's *what* is being thrown), so it's the **direct object**.

**Choose whether the bolded part of the sentence is the subject, direct object, or indirect object.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

* A **subject** is the noun that *performs* the verb; it's the *who*.

 *Adam baked.*

 **Adam** is the **subject**.

* A **direct object** is the noun being *acted* upon; it's the *what*

 *Adam baked a cake.*

 **Cake** is the **direct object**.

* An **indirect object** is the noun *receiving* the action; it's the *for whom*.

 *Adam baked Martha a cake.*

 **Martha** is the **indirect object**.
The bolded noun is the recipient of the action (i.e., *for whom* the action is done), so it's the **indirect object**.
